content:
https://github.com/wedsonaf/linux/blob/8ab4c99d510bdc99703093ad9afb649de809c104/context.md

What do you all think?
Is this truly zero overhead? &mut impl Trait is still going to pass around a pointer to something, right?

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

https://github.com/wedsonaf/linux/blob/8ab4c99d510bdc99703093ad9afb649de809c104/context.md

What do you all think?

Should atomic(), raw_atomic() and sleepable() be unsafe? Calling them means the callers need to know the context guarantees

It is zero-cost, for example, this code:

pub struct Token;

extern "C" {
    fn external();
}

fn test1(a: &mut Token) {
    unsafe { external() };
}

pub fn test() {
    test1(&mut Token);
}

Compiles to:

example::test:
        b       external

For aarch64. Link to godbolt: https://rust.godbolt.org/z/x39caEh5d

Boqun Feng said:

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

https://github.com/wedsonaf/linux/blob/8ab4c99d510bdc99703093ad9afb649de809c104/context.md

What do you all think?

Should atomic(), raw_atomic() and sleepable() be unsafe? Calling them means the callers need to know the context guarantees

Yes, they should. In fact, I mention that in the text:

They [the fucntions above] will also be marked unsafe so entrypoints will need to justify why their creations are acceptable.

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

Boqun Feng said:

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

https://github.com/wedsonaf/linux/blob/8ab4c99d510bdc99703093ad9afb649de809c104/context.md

What do you all think?

Should atomic(), raw_atomic() and sleepable() be unsafe? Calling them means the callers need to know the context guarantees

Yes, they should. In fact, I mention that in the text:

They [the fucntions above] will also be marked unsafe so entrypoints will need to justify why their creations are acceptable.

Ah, sorry I missed that.
Plus, we can add preemption count into them ;-) so runtime checking
Maybe you can mention lockdep_wait_type in your document, looks to me, contexts are these wait types at compile time.

Beside, while we compare contexts with wait types, maybe we can use better names for the lifetimes in lock(), for example:

pub fn lock<'locked, 'outer, 'inner, OuterCtx: context::AsRawAtomic>(
    &'locked self,
    cx: &'outer mut OuterCtx,
) -> (&'inner mut OuterCtx::RawAtomic, Guard<'locked, Self, WriteLock>)
where
    'outer: 'inner,  // outer context outlives inner context
    'inner: 'locked, // inner context outlives the locked effect
With that, I'm confident to explain this to Peterz ;-)
Ah, and as_atomic() and as_raw_atomic() can also have lockdep runtime checking!

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

https://github.com/wedsonaf/linux/blob/8ab4c99d510bdc99703093ad9afb649de809c104/context.md

What do you all think?

This is great! I think this looks quite lean, although time will tell how easy it is to use in practice. Carrying the info implicitly would be easier to work with, but I sort of agree with Linus arguments of why not to do that.

I think there is a typo at the end. Should it be "fleshed out" instead of "flushed out"?

This abstraction is not zero-cost. If the compiler doesn't see both caller and callee at the same time (or if the callee may be called by a caller outside of the current cgu), it is not able to remove the reference argument. What would be zero-cost would be to have something like struct Sleepable<'a>(PhantomData<&'a mut ()>);, but this has the disadvantage that you need an explicit reborrow method that you must call where the compiler would otherwise implicitly reborrow the mutable reference.

Boqun Feng said:

Plus, we can add preemption count into them ;-) so runtime checking

Noooo :)

Boqun Feng said:

Maybe you can mention lockdep_wait_type in your document, looks to me, contexts are these wait types at compile time.

I'm not as familiar with lockdep as you are, so I can't make this connection, if you want to add to the doc, I'd be happy to accept contributions :)

Boqun Feng said:

Beside, while we compare contexts with wait types, maybe we can use better names for the lifetimes in lock(), for example:

pub fn lock<'locked, 'outer, 'inner, OuterCtx: context::AsRawAtomic>(
    &'locked self,
    cx: &'outer mut OuterCtx,
) -> (&'inner mut OuterCtx::RawAtomic, Guard<'locked, Self, WriteLock>)
where
    'outer: 'inner,  // outer context outlives inner context
    'inner: 'locked, // inner context outlives the locked effect

This indeed does look easier to read.

Andreas Hindborg said:

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

https://github.com/wedsonaf/linux/blob/8ab4c99d510bdc99703093ad9afb649de809c104/context.md

What do you all think?

This is great! I think this looks quite lean, although time will tell how easy it is to use in practice. Carrying the info implicitly would be easier to work with, but I sort of agree with Linus arguments of why not to do that.

I think there is a typo at the end. Should it be "fleshed out" instead of "flushed out"?

Thanks! Indeed it should be fleshed, I'll update it :)
Use of impl for this case would cause code bloat and very slow compilation.
Because this will turn all otherwise non-generic functions into generic ones and then it will be codegenned in use site instead of def site.

bjorn3 said:

This abstraction is not zero-cost. If the compiler doesn't see both caller and callee at the same time (or if the callee may be called by a caller outside of the current cgu), it is not able to remove the reference argument. What would be zero-cost would be to have something like struct Sleepable<'a>(PhantomData<&'a mut ()>);, but this has the disadvantage that you need an explicit reborrow method that you must call where the compiler would otherwise implicitly reborrow the mutable reference.

What exactly makes the above not zero-cost?

I'd prefer to avoid having explicit types because that would make it harder for us to extend the types of contexts. For example, if we have to have a specific atomic type that is running in softirq, we could define a new type that implements both Atomic and SoftIrq, and then functions that allow Atomic contexts would still be accessible, and we would still be able to have functions that are only allowed from SoftIrq contexts.

Gary Guo said:

Use of impl for this case would cause code bloat and very slow compilation.

Hmm. I don't follow how this will generate code bloat: if a generic function is used with only one type, then it will only be instantiated once. Even if it's used with more than one type but they all result in the same code (because the type is eventually just ignored), then the compiler should be able to fold everything into one instance, no?

I am concerned about having something sound, zero-cost, as ergonomic as we can reasonably get it, and extensible. Once we settle on that, the compiler being slow is, to me, just a compiler implementation issue.

What exactly makes the above not zero-cost?

The fact that there is an extra pointer as argument when LLVM is unable to remove it due to not all callers being visible when codegening the callee.

Generic instantiation happens when the functions are called. If multiple codegen units use a generic function, this function will be duplicated by each codegen unit. The compiler is not able to fold them together without LTO. Only the linker can do so, which it only does if optimizations didn't change a single byte between instances and the linker supports it in the first place and you enable the option to do this.

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

Boqun Feng said:

Maybe you can mention lockdep_wait_type in your document, looks to me, contexts are these wait types at compile time.

I'm not as familiar with lockdep as you are, so I can't make this connection, if you want to add to the doc, I'd be happy to accept contributions :)

I can certainly do that

I really like the idea of tracking execution context, but I think it should be some kind of Rust language feature that makes it implicit (or more ergonomic to use). Carrying around a cx everywhere might be okay for simple drivers, but having to throw it into every single function call of a complex driver is really a huge usability problem... and even if you can elide it in some code paths that don't call back into kernel core functions, the moment you add one you have to add the argument to the entire call chain to that point.

Linus is against runtime tracking, but not implicit compile-time tracking! I think this should be a Rust compile-time feature request ^^. I'd like to see something like having "sleepable" be the default context, and then functions that promise to be callable from Atomic/RawAtomic should be marked as such via an attribute or something like that (and then can only call functions that also have those attributes). You still need to mark call chains up (no real way around that) and you still need some sort of mechanism to convert the current context when you actually need to make a change, but at least this way you can avoid adding the argument to all function calls...

For reference, the GPU driver I'm working on currently has 304 instances of fn, and I actually don't think there's a single atomic-context call anywhere, since GPU drivers can pretty much always sleep. I really don't want to have to add context arguments to everything... the problem with this argument approach is that the default in the kernel for most drivers is Sleepable (least restrictive), while the only functions that can be called in any context are those that are RawAtomic (most restrictive), and those are the only ones which could elide the context argument. So you end up with the common case being "carry a context everywhere", and I think that's really bad for usability...

Also, how would this work for implicit calls, like Drop? I have Drop implementations that call into the kernel, but I can't pass a context into those... If it's an implicit mechanism it could be made to work, but then it would have to be more deeply built into the type system (propagate upwards through wrapper types somehow).
Relevant topic: https://tmandry.gitlab.io/blog/posts/2021-12-21-context-capabilities/

We are open to any language feature, but unfortunately none exists right now (even behind a feature gate). So we have to have some answers with what we have today, and it's kinda a chicken-and-egg issue: first if there is no real usage, there would be no feature, and on the other hand, if there is no feature, nothing we can use.

Besides, for any language feature, there must be some transformation of the code that could explain the feature, e.g. async actually does a CPS transformation for the function and block. What Wedson proposed is the basic we want behind any future feature that can help us. Consider this as a way to talk to Rust language team, not only we tell them that we want something, but also the thing is described in Rust code so that they can understand the exact requirement.

One thing I have in my mind is whether we can use proc_marco to make life easier, for example, a function needs a Sleepable context can be written as:

#[Context(Sleepable)]
pub fn foo<'a>(arg1: T1, arg2: T2) -> T3<'a> { .. }

Can proc_marco generate an extra function like:

pub fn
__with_context_foo<'a, 'ctx>(arg1: T1, arg2: T2, cx: &'outer mut Sleepable) ->  T3 <'a>
where 'ctx: 'a,
{
.. // the function body of `foo`
}

?

And reimplement foo as:

// assume_sleepable() is `unsafe`
pub unsafe fn foo<'a>(arg1: T1, arg2: T2) -> T3<'a> {
    __with_context_foo(arg1, arg2, &mut assume_sleepable())
}

In other words, if you directly call foo, it's unsafe. Whenever a sleepable context is given, you can call foo with a macro:

with_context!(ctx, foo(arg1, arg2))

and with_context is safe.

Or forget the unsafe trick,

#[Context(Sleepable)]
pub fn foo<'a>(arg1: T1, arg2: T2) -> T3<'a> { .. }

just generates a

pub fn foo<'a, 'ctx>(arg1: T1, arg2: T2, cx: &'outer mut Sleepable) ->  T3 <'a>
where 'ctx: 'a { .. }

small nitpick: I would want the sleepable token to be a ZST instead of a reference (and thus zero-cost).

I am not really sure if marking functions that are allowed to sleep will be a good idea... At the moment the majority of functions seem to allow sleeping, so almost every function would end up with #[context(Sleepable)]. You could also create a #[no_sleep] attribute that adds a NoSleep token to the signature. But making this design be sound seems challenging (with lang support this would be very similar to const, but I do not think that such a specific keyword is going to be added without a more general system behind to create these kinds of attributes).
Yes, lots of functions allow sleeping, but only part of them might sleep, if a function don't need to sleep, it won't need the #[context(Sleepable)]. Of course, always open to better naming ;-)

Maybe an example of the questions that this tries to solve can help the discussion, in kernel it's illegal to write code as:

rcu_read_lock();
some_function_might_sleep();
rcu_read_unlock();

it's deadlock when PREEMPT=y and UAF when PREEMPT=n, and if we were to expose the RCU api, without the context tracking thing, we would have make either rcu_read_lock() or some_funtion_might_sleep() unsafe per Rust safety requirement. Neither way looks good.. note that we have many similar issues. How will #[no_sleep] help this situation?

Boqun Feng said:

We are open to any language feature, but unfortunately none exists right now (even behind a feature gate). So we have to have some answers with what we have today, and it's kinda a chicken-and-egg issue: first if there is no real usage, there would be no feature, and on the other hand, if there is no feature, nothing we can use.

Besides, for any language feature, there must be some transformation of the code that could explain the feature, e.g. async actually does a CPS transformation for the function and block. What Wedson proposed is the basic we want behind any future feature that can help us. Consider this as a way to talk to Rust language team, not only we tell them that we want something, but also the thing is described in Rust code so that they can understand the exact requirement.

One thing I have in my mind is whether we can use proc_marco to make life easier, for example, a function needs a Sleepable context can be written as:

#[Context(Sleepable)]
pub fn foo<'a>(arg1: T1, arg2: T2) -> T3<'a> { .. }

Can proc_marco generate an extra function like:

pub fn

where 'ctx: 'a,

?

And reimplement foo as:

// assume_sleepable() is `unsafe`
pub unsafe fn foo<'a>(arg1: T1, arg2: T2) -> T3<'a> {

In other words, if you directly call foo, it's unsafe. Whenever a sleepable context is given, you can call foo with a macro:

with_context!(ctx, foo(arg1, arg2))

and with_context is safe.

I had a very similar idea of annotating functions with context information, except that it would have more information, e.g., the at_least(sleepable), returns(atomic), etc.). And I hadn't considered making an unsafe version available, my thought there was to eventually make sleepable and friends public and unsafe, so one could always call functions by unsafely manufacturing a context if needed; but I do like the idea of having an unsafe counter-part if we're generating code anyway.

I had a harder time automatically passing context though, because of the cases when it changes. If for example, we acquire a spinlock then a raw-spinlock, then we release the spinlock, we stay in raw-atomic until the raw-spinlock is released, at which point we go to whatever the context was when the first spinlock was acquired (atomic or sleepable) -- this all works well with the borrow checker, but we would need to have that level of understanding of the code and lifetimes in order to pass the right context. So although it's possible to do it, it seems too complicated to implement in a proc macro...

y86 said:

small nitpick: I would want the sleepable token to be a ZST instead of a reference (and thus zero-cost).

I am not really sure if marking functions that are allowed to sleep will be a good idea... At the moment the majority of functions seem to allow sleeping, so almost every function would end up with #[context(Sleepable)]. You could also create a #[no_sleep] attribute that adds a NoSleep token to the signature. But making this design be sound seems challenging (with lang support this would be very similar to const, but I do not think that such a specific keyword is going to be added without a more general system behind to create these kinds of attributes).

I see two problems with a true ZST solution: (a) it needs a reborrow every time we need a call (if we want to keep it to use later) so it complicates the usage even further, though admittedly not insurmountably; and (b) we will probably need callees to return the ZST "token" again (we can use an invariant lifetime to ensure the same one is returned) but this complicates the usage even more, but if we don't do this , the callee could stash the zst token away and later reuse it wrongly (and unsoundly) without any unsafe code.

+1 to Boqun's point wrt to the meaning of the annotation: it's not "I'm ok to sleep", it's "I may have to sleep, so you had better call me from a sleepable context"!

Boqun Feng said:

How will #[no_sleep] help this situation?

Yeah your design seems more suitable for this. rcu_read_lock would probably need to take a #[no_sleep] closure...

small nitpick: I would want the sleepable token to be a ZST instead of a reference (and thus zero-cost).

I am not really sure if marking functions that are allowed to sleep will be a good idea... At the moment the majority of functions seem to allow sleeping, so almost every function would end up with #[context(Sleepable)]. You could also create a #[no_sleep] attribute that adds a NoSleep token to the signature. But making this design be sound seems challenging (with lang support this would be very similar to const, but I do not think that such a specific keyword is going to be added without a more general system behind to create these kinds of attributes).

I see two problems with a true ZST solution: (a) it needs a reborrow every time we need a call (if we want to keep it to use later) so it complicates the usage even further, though admittedly not insurmountably; and (b) we will probably need callees to return the ZST "token" again (we can use an invariant lifetime to ensure the same one is returned) but this complicates the usage even more, but if we don't do this , the callee could stash the zst token away and later reuse it wrongly (and unsoundly) without any unsafe code.

+1 to Boqun's point wrt to the meaning of the annotation: it's not "I'm ok to sleep", it's "I may have to sleep, so you had better call me from a sleepable context"!

(a) wouldnt the reborrow be done by the macro?
(b) I think this could be done with closures:

mod token {
    use core::marker::PhantomData;
    // our invariant token
    pub struct Token<'a>(PhantomData<fn(&'a ()) -> &'a ()>);

    impl<'a> Token<'a> {
        // use the token in the closure and return the same token again + some retvalue
        pub fn use_token<'b, R>(&'b mut self, f: impl FnOnce(Token<'b>) -> RetToken<'b, R>) -> R
        where
            'a: 'b,
        {
            f(Token(PhantomData)).0
        }

        pub unsafe fn conjure() -> Self {
            Self(PhantomData)
        }

        pub fn sleep(&self, millis: u64) {
            std::thread::sleep(std::time::Duration::from_millis(millis));
        }
    }

    pub struct RetToken<'a, R>(pub R, pub Token<'a>);
}

use token::*;

macro_rules! ctx {
    ($tok:ident, $f:ident($($arg:expr),*$(,)?)) => {
        $tok.use_token(move |tok| $f(tok, $($arg),*))
    }
}

fn foo<'a>(tok: Token<'a>, typ: i32) -> RetToken<'a, i32> {
    tok.sleep(10);
    RetToken(42 + typ, tok)
}

fn bar<'a>(mut tok: Token<'a>) -> RetToken<'a, i32> {
    tok.sleep(100);
    RetToken(ctx!(tok, foo(42)), tok)
}

fn main() {
    let mut tok = unsafe { Token::conjure() };
    println!("{}", ctx!(tok, foo(0)));
    println!("{}", ctx!(tok, bar()));
}

the ctx! macro is just for demonstration, it should be done by the #[context(Sleepable)] proc-macro by recursing into every function call of the function and add the tok parameter. This seems a little bit too complex atm, especially because we do not have syn...

especially because we do not have syn...

While this is true at the moment, if a solution would be made "reasonable to implement" by having syn or similar, we could still consider it, i.e. I wouldn't discard it completely just because of that.

(We talked about this in Kangrejos, but I put it here to clarify it for others)

Please don't forget about the Drop issue. I don't see how you could work around not being able to pass the context into Drop impls without using global state, and it would break many use cases in my driver (half the point of using Rust is to manage memory implicitly, and that means calling cleanup/free/unmap kernel functions from Drop impls!).

I think we really do need a language feature here...

@Boqun Feng to come back to your example with #[context(Sleepable)]. How would you prevent the following?

#[context(Sleepable)]
pub fn bar() {
    ctx!(foo(arg1, arg2));
    // do something such that we are not allowed to sleep any longer
    rcu_read_lock();
    // now do something that might sleep...
    ctx!(foo(arg1, arg2));
}

I believe the proc macro would need to modify every function call to prevent this.

Good question!
Basically for this we need Generator or CPS transformation, IIUC.

The reborrow is needed if you want to preserve some previous context. Your solution does't address the case where the context changes momentarily, then goes back to the previous one. For example, if you're in sleepable mode and acquire a spinlock, you transition to atomic context; once you release the lock, you go back to sleepable.

Now, if you were already in atomic context and then acquired a spinlock, it would remain in atomic context even after release the spinlock.

Things get more complicated if you have several transitions within the same scope (e.g., acquiring a mutexes, spin locks, raw spinlocks) and releasing in unconventional order (i.e., not in reverse order).

Boqun Feng said:

Basically for this we need Generator or CPS transformation, IIUC.

Why generator? I think a natural proc macro should suffice. I am writing a small demo right now...

Yeah the problem is a bit more involved... I will see how far the simple idea gets me and then see what else is needed.

y86 said:

Boqun Feng said:

Basically for this we need Generator or CPS transformation, IIUC.

Why generator? I think a natural proc macro should suffice. I am writing a small demo right now...

Please make sure you cover the scenarios I describe above :)

I am personally against the use of tokens for sleepable contexts. It'll be a huge usability issue. You'll have to carry the token everywhere, and it'll get more complicated when a closure is involved.

What I have in mind is to implement a custom linter for this. Before we have the config option to remove OOM panic handling, I demonstrated a linter to detect fallible allocation (https://github.com/nbdd0121/klint), and I think this would be doable for contexts too.

We could use a combination of optional runtime checking (like current might_sleep) plus the linting to detect problems. Sure, both the runtime checking and the linting could be disabled, but I believe this is a case where kernel needs trump Rust need scenario as Linus discussed in the mailing list.

So, I have tried to implement it and it is just not a great experience.

Boqun Feng said:

y86 said:

Boqun Feng to come back to your example with #[context(Sleepable)]. How would you prevent the following?

#[context(Sleepable)]
pub fn bar() {
    ctx!(foo(arg1, arg2));
    // do something such that we are not allowed to sleep any longer

I believe the proc macro would need to modify every function call to prevent this.

Good question!

This problem also occurs with macros (if we call rcu_read_lock via a macro) and that is something a proc macro cannot do anything about.

I think going with the linting route like @Gary Guo suggested seems a lot more manageable -- at least in the short term.

Say we go with linter, should we mark rcu_read_lock() and do_something_might_sleep() as safe? If so, then the following is still unsound but we don't care because linter says "I have warned you"?

rcu_read_lock();
do_something_might_sleep(); // linter will warn about calling a might_sleep function inside RCU read-side C.S.

y86 said:

I agree, it could be very similar to an unsafe block:

fn foo() {
    no_sleep {
        rcu_read_lock();
        some_function_might_sleep();
//      ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ error calling non `no_sleep` function in `no_sleep` block!
        rcu_read_unlock();
    }
}

This is too simplistic.

rcu_read_lock, if called from sleepable context will transition the context to atomic; if called from atomic, will transition remain atomic; if called from raw-atomic, will remain raw-atomic. Once the guard is released, it goes back to the previous state, unless what took you to that state has already been released as well, in which case you have to go back further.

I think this can be modelled as a ref-counted stack. Elements of the stack with a zero count are popped.

This is too simplistic.

rcu_read_lock, if called from sleepable context will transition the context to atomic; if called from atomic, will transition remain atomic; if called from raw-atomic, will remain raw-atomic. Once the guard is released, it goes back to the previous state, unless what took you to that state has already been released as well, in which case you have to go back further.

I think this can be modelled as a ref-counted stack. Elements of the stack with a zero count are popped.

ok that makes sense. And there is the need for contexts to be left in non reverse order, right? So without that some features would just flat out not work?

We need to allow for leaving context in non reverse order. In my stack idea, this would result in an element of the stack being decremented (possibly to zero) that isn't the top, so the current context doesn't change. When the top does go to zero, it gets popped; then if whatever is at the top now is already zero, it gets popped too, until we reach a non-zero element (or stack is empty).

rcu::read_lock is special in the sense that it may increment an element of the stack that isn't the top. For example, if we're already in raw-atomic, then we increment the stack entry for atomic and continue in raw-atomic mode.

right, but we would still need some kind of object that has the lifetime of the context attached to it. IIRC there was some reason that rcu could not have a guard, but I do not remember... I do not think that any feature will support "this function will enter this mode" and "this function will leave this mode" safely.

My first instinct when I saw the problem is to use ZST "tokens" and move semantics, but the mutable reference context solution looks much more ergonomic because you automatically get the original context back when the borrow ends, whereas with tokens they have to be returned explicitly.

But like @bjorn3 said mutable references add an extra point argument, so probably not zero-cost. Using ZST move tokens wouldn't have this problem, right?
Yes, ZST's are entirely omitted in the rust abi.
And the C abi on most platforms also omits them.

For ZST tokens you can also have a reborrow method taking &mut Token and returning a token of a shorter lifetime. If this reborrow method is marked as inline it should be optimized out and if you use the correct lifetimes in it's signature, the "borrowed" token will be inaccessible until the returned token is gone.
Ah, nice! That solves the ergonomics problem. And I think rust already has a lot of those reborrow methods, so this is idiomatic as well.

I think we have a problem with using lifetimes in either case: we are assuming that once the lifetime of the new "derived" context ends, the original one is usable again. This isn't always true. If, for example, the outer context is sleepable, and an inner one is atomic because we acquired a spin lock, if we forget the guard, it will end the lifetime of the atomic context, rendering the sleepable one usable again; but since we failed to unlock the spinlock, we're still in atomic context. Using the (now accessible) sleepable context may cause UB.

So this modelling of contexts using lifetimes is unfortunately unsound.
Right

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

Note quite. I think lifetimes can handle this. The problem is when we don't unlock.

Problem with not unlocking is trivially addressed with an API that takes closure. But as you said this is not as flexible.

Not unlocking can also be prevented using (declarative) macros:

macro_rules! guard {
    ($a: ident) => {
        let mut $a: Guard = unsafe { Guard::new() };
        let $a: Pin<&mut Guard> = unsafe { Pin::new(&mut $a) };
    }
}

This prevents forgetting $a because the identifier has been shadowed by a mutable reference to $a, so there is no way to refer to it.
